coredata.dc:title: Facial fabrication of carbon quantum dots (CDs)-modified N-TiO2-x nanocomposite for the efficient photoreduction of Cr(VI) under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Lu
Bai, Xue
Guo, Linkai
Yang, Shengjiong
Jin, Pengkang
Yang, Lei
coredata.dc:description: In this study, for the first time, a novel CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was successfully fabricated by decorating the carbon quantum dots (CDs) on the nitrogen (N) and Ti3+ codoped TiO2 nanoparticles using a facile hydrothermal-calcination method. X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), etc., were utilized to investigate the physicochemical properties of the catalyst and to confirm the successful introduction of CDs and the formation of N/Ti3+ codoping levels in the nanocomposite. Owing to the synergy between the modification of CDs and codoping of N and Ti3+, the as-prepared CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite exhibited enhanced visible light absorption and more efficient charge separation compared with its counterparts, such as the pristine TiO2, N-doped TiO2 (N-TiO2), N and Ti3+ codoped TiO2 (N-TiO2-x), and carbon quantum dots-TiO2 (CDs-TiO2), etc. As a result, the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite displayed a remarkable performance for Cr(VI) reduction under visible light irradiation. Moreover, adding the appropriate amount of citric acid as the hole scavenger significantly increased the reduction rate of Cr(VI). In addition, the reduction efficiency was better under acidic conditions and considerably affected by the initial Cr(VI) concentration and catalyst dosage. Thermogravimetric analysis and cyclic runs demonstrated a unique thermal and chemical stability of the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite. The mechanism of the enhanced photoreduction of Cr(VI) by the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was discussed based on all the characterization and experimental results. Moreover, the developed CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite also exhibited efficient photocatalytic performance to reduce the Cr(VI) contained in real laboratory wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Visible light photocatalysis
Carbon quantum dots (CDs)
Ti3+ and nitrogen codoped TiO2
CDs-N-TiO2-x
Cr(VI) reduction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of pristine TiO2, N-TiO2 and carbon quantum dots (CDs)
Thermal and chemical stability of the catalyst
Application in the treatment of real laboratory wastewater

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD patterns of pristine TiO2 (black curve), N-TiO2 (orange curve), N-TiO2-x (sky blue curve), 5% CDs-TiO2 (green curve), and 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x (blue curve). (B) FT-IR spectra of CDs (black curve), pristine TiO2 (orange curve), N-TiO2-x (sky blue curve), and 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x (green curve). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Low resolution and (B) high resolution TEM image of the N-TiO2-x nanocomposite, inset of Fig. 2A: particle size distributions of the N-TiO2-x nanocomposite. (C) Low resolution and (D) high resolution TEM image of the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite, inset of Fig. 2C: particle size distributions of CDs in the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite. (E) STEM image and (F)–(I) elemental mapping images of the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite.

(A) Photographs of the as-prepared photocatalysts. (B) The diffuse reflectance UV–vis spectra of all the photocatalysts. (C) Photoluminescence spectra of CDs. (D) EPR spectra of the as-prepared photocatalysts. (E) Valence band XPS spectra of all the photocatalysts. (F) Energy band structure diagram of pristine TiO2 and 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite.

(A) C 1s, (B) Ti 2p, (C) O 1s, and (D) N 1s XPS spectra of pristine TiO2 and 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite. The black dots are original XPS data. The yellow curves are the fitting results, which can be split into several peaks (shown in the other colors). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Comparison of Cr(VI) reduction efficiencies by different catalysts under visible-light irradiation. (B) Adsorption amount of Cr(VI) by various catalysts after the adsorption-desorption equilibrium was established. (C) The corresponding reaction rate constants during the Cr(VI) reduction process by different catalysts. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of aqueous Cr(VI) treated by the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite under visible light irradiation. (E) Remaining ratio of Cr(VI) and total Cr during photoreduction over the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite under visible light irradiation. (F) Remaining ratio of Cr(VI) and the total Cr during adsorption in the dark by the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite. Photoreduction reaction conditions: Cr(VI) concentration = 10 mg/L, pH = 5.36, catalyst dosage = 1.0 g/L. light intensity = 2150 ± 10 w/m2.

(A) Effect of a hole scavenger on Cr(VI) reduction; (B) the corresponding reaction rate constant during Cr(VI) reduction by 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x under visible light irradiation. Reaction conditions: Cr(VI) concentration = 10 mg/L, pH = 5.36, catalyst dosage = 1.0 g/L, light intensity = 2150 ± 10 w/m2.

(A) Effect of initial substrate concentration on Cr(VI) reduction; (B) the corresponding reaction rate constant during Cr(VI) reduction by 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x under visible light irradiation. Reaction conditions: catalyst dosage = 1.0 g/L, pH = 5.36, citric acid concentration = 1.0 mM, light intensity = 2150 ± 10 w/m2.

(A) Effect of catalyst dosage on Cr(VI) reduction; (B) the corresponding reaction rate constant during Cr(VI) reduction by 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x under visible light irradiation. Reaction conditions: Cr(VI) concentration = 10 mg/L, pH = 5.36, citric acid concentration = 1.0 mM, light intensity = 2150 ± 10 w/m2.

(A) Effect of initial solution pH on Cr(VI) reduction; (B) the corresponding reaction rate constant during Cr(VI) reduction by 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x under visible light irradiation. Inset of (B) Zeta potential of 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x in the pH range of 2–10. Reaction conditions: Cr(VI) concentration = 10 mg/L, catalyst dosage = 1.0 g/L, citric acid concentration = 1.0 mM, light intensity = 2150 ± 10 w/m2.

(A) TGA curve of the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite. (B) Cyclic tests of Cr(VI) reduction by 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite under visible light irradiation. Reaction conditions: Cr(VI) concentration = 10 mg/L, pH = 5.36, catalyst dosage = 1.0 g/L, citric acid concentration = 1.0 mM, light intensity = 2150 ± 10 w/m2.

(A) PL spectra, (B) time-resolved PL spectra, and (C) transient photocurrent responses of the different photocatalysts at bias 0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) under visible light. (D) The proposed enhanced photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) by the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite under visible light irradiation.

Photoreduction efficiencies of Cr(VI) in real laboratory wastewater by pristine TiO2, N-TiO2-x, 5% CDs-TiO2, and 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposites under visible light irradiation. Reaction conditions: catalyst dosage = 2.0 g/L, citric acid concentration = 1.0 mM, light intensity = 2150 ± 10 w/m2.
Illustration of the preparation process of the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite.
Comparison of Cr(VI) reduction by different catalysts under visible light.

head.ce:title: Facial fabrication of carbon quantum dots (CDs)-modified N-TiO2-x nanocomposite for the efficient photoreduction of Cr(VI) under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Northwest Water Resources, Environment and Ecology, Ministry of Education, School of Environmental and Municipal Engineering, Xi’an University of Architecture and Technology, Xi’an, Shaanxi Province 710055, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Northwest Water Resources, Environment and Ecology
School of Environmental and Municipal Engineering
Xi’an University of Architecture and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, for the first time, a novel CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was successfully fabricated by decorating the carbon quantum dots (CDs) on the nitrogen (N) and Ti3+ codoped TiO2 nanoparticles using a facile hydrothermal-calcination method. X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), etc., were utilized to investigate the physicochemical properties of the catalyst and to confirm the successful introduction of CDs and the formation of N/Ti3+ codoping levels in the nanocomposite. Owing to the synergy between the modification of CDs and codoping of N and Ti3+, the as-prepared CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite exhibited enhanced visible light absorption and more efficient charge separation compared with its counterparts, such as the pristine TiO2, N-doped TiO2 (N-TiO2), N and Ti3+ codoped TiO2 (N-TiO2-x), and carbon quantum dots-TiO2 (CDs-TiO2), etc. As a result, the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite displayed a remarkable performance for Cr(VI) reduction under visible light irradiation. Moreover, adding the appropriate amount of citric acid as the hole scavenger significantly increased the reduction rate of Cr(VI). In addition, the reduction efficiency was better under acidic conditions and considerably affected by the initial Cr(VI) concentration and catalyst dosage. Thermogravimetric analysis and cyclic runs demonstrated a unique thermal and chemical stability of the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite. The mechanism of the enhanced photoreduction of Cr(VI) by the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was discussed based on all the characterization and experimental results. Moreover, the developed CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite also exhibited efficient photocatalytic performance to reduce the Cr(VI) contained in real laboratory wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the wide use of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) in metal finishing, steel fabrication and other industries [1], the difficulty posed by Cr(VI)-containing wastewater has become the focus of industrial wastewater treatment. Compared with the highly toxic Cr(VI) species, trivalent chromium (Cr(III)) is nontoxic and can be easily removed from the aqueous phase via Cr(OH)3 precipitation under neutral or alkaline conditions [2]. Therefore, a two-stage reduction-precipitation process is always utilized to remove Cr(VI) from aquatic environments. Hence, much effort has been focused on the development of efficient Cr(VI) reduction technologies [3], including the chemical reduction process [4,5], electrochemical reduction process [6,7], biological reduction process [8–10], and photocatalytic reduction process [11–14].

Among the numerous technologies, the photocatalytic process, especially visible light photoreduction of Cr(VI), has attracted extensive research interest, due to its low cost, excellent performance, and the potential sustainability of green solar energy [11–16]. During this process, a photocatalyst absorbs the photons with the energies matching its band gap, leading to the production of electrons (e−) and holes (h+). After effective charge separation, e− in the conduction band migrate to the surface of the catalyst and further react with Cr(VI) and reduce it [3]. Therefore, promoting the light absorption and charge separation of the catalyst are important for the efficient reduction of Cr(VI) over the photocatalytic process. As a result, many fruitful studies have been devoted to achieving the above objectives, and several emerging photocatalytic materials have been explored, such as WO3-TiO2 [11], AgI/TiO2 [17], AgI/BiOI-Bi2O3 [18], Ag-Ag2S/TiO2 [19], g-C3N4/SnS2/SnO2 [20], α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 [21], ZnIn2S4/CdS [22], Cd0.5Zn0.5S@ZIF-8 [23], etc. However, issues such as the high cost, harsh and complicated preparation, and limited-utilization efficiency of visible light severely retard the practical applications of these photocatalysts.

Recently, new carbon quantum dots (CDs)-TiO2 nanosheets (CDs-TNs) with an enhanced visible light response was developed [24,25]. Due to the unique photon conversion and electron transfer properties of carbon quantum dots (CDs) [26–30], the performance of visible light photoreduction of Cr(VI) by CDs-TNs was effectively improved. However, the photoluminescence emission of CDs is mainly located at 430–550 nm [24]; in fact, the photon conversion performance of CDs cannot be efficiently utilized in the reported CDs-TNs due to the narrow absorption range of pristine TiO2 (<400 nm). Therefore, there is a pressing need to develop a facile strategy for decreasing the band gap of the nanocomposite carrier matching the photoluminescence of CDs. Recently, owing to their superior visible light photocatalytic performance, nitrogen (N) and Ti3+ codoped TiO2 (N-TiO2-x) photocatalysts have attracted more attention [31–33]. In particular, Zhang et al. [31] utilized a simple activation method to prepare the N-TiO2-x nanocomposite. The formation of N doping levels above the valence band and Ti3+/Ov doping levels beneath the conduction band in the nanocomposite is beneficial for its band gap narrowing. As a consequence, the light absorption and harvesting of the catalyst can be improved significantly.

Inspired by the abovementioned excellent works, we developed a facile two-step hydrothermal and calcination synthetic strategy (Scheme 1 ) to fabricate the novel carbon quantum dots (CDs) decorated, nitrogen (N) and Ti3+/Ov codoped TiO2 nanocomposite (CDs-N-TiO2-x). The photon conversion function of CDs could be more efficiently utilized by introducing N and Ti3+ doping levels into the CDs-TiO2 composite. In turn, the introduction of CDs not only improved the light absorption ability but also effectively promoted the charge separation of the N-TiO2-x nanocomposite. As a consequence, the photocatalytic activities of CDs-N-TiO2-x on the reduction of Cr(VI) could be significantly improved compared to the reported CDs-TiO2 and N-TiO2-x nanocomposites. The crystal structure, morphology, optical property, and chemical composition of the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite were investigated via several characterizations. Furthermore, the effects of a hole scavenger, initial substrate concentration, catalyst dosage, and the solution pH were investigated in depth. Then, a feasible mechanism for the outstanding performance on Cr(VI) reduction by the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was proposed in light of the experimental results. Moreover, the as-prepared CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite can effectively treat the real wastewater produced in a laboratory with 100% Cr(VI) reduction efficiency, demonstrating that the novel CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite possesses a great potential for remediating Cr(VI)-containing wastewater.

Urea, absolute ethanol (EtOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), titanium butoxide (TBOT), citric acid, potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), acetone, and diphenylcarbazide (DPC) were all purchased from the Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pristine TiO2, N-TiO2 and carbon quantum dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-TiO2 was prepared by a hydrothermal method [32]. In brief, 400 mg urea was fully dissolved in 50 mL of EtOH. Then, 2 mL of HCl and 8 mL of TBOT were added to the above solution and stirred for 2 h. Next, the final mixture was transferred to a high-pressure autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 4 h. The final substrates were washed by EtOH and ultrapure water several times and then dried at 60 °C, and the obtained powders were named precursor-N-TiO2 (p-N-TiO2). Finally, the as-prepared p-N-TiO2 was calcined at 300 °C (air atmosphere) for 3 h to obtain the N-TiO2 composite. The pristine TiO2 was prepared by the same method without adding urea, and N-TiO2-x composite was prepared under similar conditions (calcined the p-N-TiO2 under an argon atmosphere).

CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method according to a previous study [28]. First, 3.0 g of citric acid and 1.0 g of urea were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water, followed by the hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 5 h. Then, the obtained substrate was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 30 min to remove the larger particles. Finally, the obtained solution was dried with a vacuum freeze-dryer overnight to achieve the CDs and stored in a dark and dry condition.

First, 25 mg of CDs was fully dissolved in 20 mL of EtOH. Then, 475 mg of p-N-TiO2 was added to the above solution and stirred to dry with a heating magnetic stirrer at 40 °C. Finally, the dry-powder was calcined at 300 °C (argon atmosphere) for 3 h to obtain the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x composite (the mass ratio of the CDs was 5%). The CDs-N-TiO2-x composites with different amounts of CDs (1%, 3% and 10%) were prepared under similar conditions. CDs-TiO2 and CDs-TiO2-x were also prepared under similar conditions (the corresponding precursors were calcined in air and Ar atmosphere, respectively).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the as-prepared catalysts were analyzed by a Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.154178 nm). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured by a spectrometer with the ATR accessories (Nicolet X700). The morphologies of the catalysts were analyzed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were recorded by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu). Photoluminescence spectra of liquid CDs were measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were recorded by a Bruker ZMXmicro-6/1 spectrometer at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were measured by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher ESCALAB Xi+) with Al-Kα radiation and all binding energies were referenced to the C 1s peak at 284.8 eV. The zeta potential of the catalyst was measured by a solid surface analyzer (SurPASSTM 3, Anton Paar GmbH). Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was performed using a TG-DSC simultaneous thermal analyzer (Mettler Toledo TGA/DSC3+) in air. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the catalysts were measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (FLS9, Edinburgh) with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. Time-resolved PL measurements were measured by a time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) system. Samples were photoexcited using a 325 nm laser beam and the detection wavelength was 400 nm. The transient photocurrent response of the as-prepared samples was recorded via an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E) under visible light irradiation using a three-electrode cell system (see Supplementary Material, Text. S1 for the details of the photoelectrode preparation).

The photocatalytic reaction system was primarily composed of a light source, a reactor, a cooling circulating water system, and a magnetic stirrer. The detailed information is found in Supplementary Material (SM, Text. S2 and Fig. S1).

First, 282.90 mg of K2Cr2O7 was dissolved in 1 L ultrapure water to obtain the stock solution (100 mg/L Cr(VI)/L), which was stored in amber glass bottles at 4 °C. The working solution (10 mg Cr(VI)/L) used during the photoreduction experiments was prepared freshly from the stock solution by dilution. For each experiment, 50 mg of the photocatalyst was added to 50 mL Cr(VI) aqueous solution and stirred in the dark for 30 min. During the reaction process, a 1 mL suspension was sampled from the reaction system at a certain time. The concentration of Cr(VI) was measured via the standard diphenylcarbazide (DPC) method (see SM, Text. S3 for details). The concentration of total Cr was measured using an atomic absorption spectrometer (ICETM 3300 AAS, Thermo Scientific).

As shown in Fig. 1 A, all the photocatalysts are indexed to the anatase phase TiO2 (PDF No. 21-1272), with the characteristic peaks at 25.3° (1 0 1), 37.8° (0 0 4), 48.0° (2 0 0), 54.0° (1 0 5), 55.0° (2 1 1), 62.7° (2 0 4), 68.9° (1 1 6), 70.3° (2 2 0), and 75.1° (2 1 5) [34]. However, the characteristic peak of CDs at 21.5° [24] was too weak to be observed in 5% CDs-TiO2 and 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x, which was primarily due to the very low amount of CDs in these composites. In addition, compared to the pristine TiO2, the XRD peaks of all other modified catalysts did not show significant shifts, indicating that the crystal phase and structure of the nanocomposite were not affected by the introduction of CDs, nitrogen and Ti3+/Ov doping levels.

The FT-IR spectra of all samples are shown in Fig. 1B. As observed in the spectra of the CDs, peaks at 3184 cm−1, 1654 cm−1, 1543 cm−1, 1390 cm−1, and 1172 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of NH, COOH, CC, CO, and COC bonds [24,30], respectively. All the TiO2-based catalysts possess strong peaks at 3000–3500 cm−1, 1635 cm−1, and 400–900 cm−1, corresponding to the OH group of the adsorbed water and the stretching vibration of TiOTi bonds, respectively [35,36]. Moreover, due to the formation of Ov in N-TiO2-x and 5% CD-N-TiO2-x nanocomposites [37], the intensity and position of OH absorption peaks at 3000–3500 cm−1 decreased and shifted significantly compared with those of pristine TiO2. In addition, some of the characteristic peaks of CDs at 1543 cm−1, 1390 cm−1 and 1172 cm−1 [24,30] are observed in the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite, which indicates that the CDs have been loaded to the N-TiO2-x composite successfully.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was utilized to observe the microstructure of the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite. As presented in Fig. 2 A, the particle size distribution of N-TiO2-x is 4.5–14 nm, and the average particle size is approximately 9.14 nm. Fig. 2B shows the high-resolution TEM image of N-TiO2-x, it is observed that the N-TiO2-x nanoparticle displayed the obvious core–shell structure. The lattice plane distance of the crystalline core is 0.35 nm, which can be indexed to the lattice plane (1 0 1) of anatase TiO2 [38]. The formation of the disordered shell is primarily caused by the introduction of Ti3+/Ov into the lattice of the nanocomposite according to the previous studies [38,39]. Fig. 2C shows the TEM image of 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite, it can be observed that CDs were successfully loaded to the surface of the N-TiO2-x nanoparticles using the developed facile synthetic method. Moreover, after measuring around 100 CDs particles in Fig. 2C, it can be concluded that the particle size distribution of the CDs is 0.6–2.4 nm, and the average particle size is approximately 1.48 nm. Fig. 2D shows the high-resolution TEM images of 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite, in can be seen that the lattice spacing of CDs is approximately 0.205 nm (inset of Fig. 2D), corresponding to the (1 0 2) planes of graphitic carbon [24]. Besides, as shown in Fig. 2D, the lattice spacing of CDs (0.205 nm) and N-TiO2-x (0.350 nm) can be observed at the same region, even overlapping each other, confirming the close interface contact between CDs and the N-TiO2-x nanoparticles. In addition, the detailed chemical composition of the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was further investigated via elemental mapping analysis. As can be observed in Fig. 2E-I, the C, N, Ti, and O elements were all distributed in the composite. These results indicated that the loading of CDs and formation of Ti3+/Ov impurity levels can be obtained simultaneously via the developed synthetic method.

As observed from the photographs of all the photocatalysts (Fig. 3 A), the modified catalysts are darker than the pristine TiO2. In particular, the color of the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposites deepened as the CDs content increased from 0 to 10%. To our knowledge, the deeper the color is, the stronger the absorption of light by the photocatalyst is [40]. As shown in Fig. 3B, the pristine TiO2 can only absorb the light with a wavelength below 391 nm. The absorption edges of the N-TiO2 and N-TiO2-x nanocomposites were extended from 391 to 407 and 441 nm, respectively. After loading the CDs, the absorption edges of the 5% CDs-TiO2 and 5% CDs-TiO2-x nanocomposites were only improved from 391 to 400 nm and 466 nm, respectively. Meanwhile, after loading the same amount of CDs to N-TiO2-x, the absorption edge of 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x was significantly increased from 441 nm to 671 nm. The photon-conversion property of the CDs was further investigated to better explain the above results. As presented in Fig. 3C, when excited by the long-wavelength light (500–900 nm), CDs undergo fluorescence in a wavelength range of 400–500 nm. However, the pristine TiO2 cannot absorb the light with a wavelength above 391 nm, resulting in a slight enhancement in the visible light harvesting for the 5% CDs-TiO2 nanocomposite. In contrast, due to the introduction of N and Ti3+/Ov impurity levels, the band gap of the composite was significantly narrowed. Thus, after loading the CDs, the utilization of the photon conversion property of the CDs in the composite could be greatly promoted. In addition, as observed in the inset of Fig. 3B, the light absorption ability of the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was greatly improved as the amount of CDs increased from 0 to 1%, 3%, 5%, and 10%, demonstrating that CDs play a very important role in extending the light response range of the photocatalysts. As we discussed earlier, the efficient narrowing of the band gap of the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite is mainly attributed to the nitrogen and Ti3+/Ov codoping levels in the lattice of TiO2. Hence, EPR and valence band XPS spectra were utilized to confirm the above conjecture. It has been reported that Ti3+ and oxygen vacancy species have characteristic peaks with a g-value of 1.94–1.99 and 2.004 [31,37,38], respectively. Therefore, EPR was utilized to confirm the formation of TiO2-x in the nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 3D, almost no signal can be found for the pristine TiO2, N-TiO2 and CDs-TiO2. Meanwhile, the EPR spectra for N-TiO2-x and CDs-N-TiO2-x exhibited the very strong signal at g = 1.99, corresponding to the paramagnetic Ti3+ and oxygen vacancy [31,37,38]. As shown in Fig. 3E, the VB edge of 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x was shifted toward a lower binding energy than that of the pristine TiO2 and located at approximately 1.63 eV below the Fermi energy. According to the empirical equation Eq. (1):
(1)
E g
= 1240 /
λ g
where Eg and λg are the band gap and adsorption edge of the catalyst, respectively. Therefore, the band gap energy of the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was calculated to be 1.85 eV. Consequently, the detailed energy band structure of the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite can be obtained. As illustrated in Fig. 3F, the conduction band position of the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was estimated to be −0.22 eV vs. NHE, which is sufficiently negative to reduce Cr(VI) (ECr(VI)/Cr(III) = +1.36 eV vs. NHE [16]).

To further verify the successful introduction of CDs and the formation of Ti3+/Ov and N codoping levels in the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite, the chemical compositions and bonding configurations of pristine TiO2 and 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x were analyzed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and the results are presented in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4A shows the high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra. The main peak of pristine TiO2 can be fitted into two Gaussian peaks at 284.62 eV and 284.93 eV, which are attributed to the CC/CC bond and the CO bond [24,41], respectively. In contrast, for the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite, there are four fitting peaks at 283.66 eV, 284.45 eV, 285.56 eV, and 288.84 eV, which are related to the TiOC bond, the CC/CC bond, the CO bond, and the CO bond [24,41], respectively. The presence of CO and TiOC bands demonstrate the presence of CDs in the nanocomposite and the chemical connection between CDs and N-TiO2-x. As shown in Fig. 4B, the binding energies of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 in the pristine TiO2 are located at 458.88 eV and 464.58 eV, which are attributed to the Ti4+-O bands [38]. In the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite, the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2 p1/2 peaks shift to the low binding energies of 458.24 eV and 463.95 eV, respectively, and can be further split into four peaks at 458.37 (Ti4+ 2p3/2), 458.05 (Ti3+ 2p3/2), 464.22 (Ti4+ 2p1/2), and 463.38 eV (Ti3+ 2p1/2), which indicate that the Ti3+ species have been successfully introduced into the nanocomposite [32,38,42]. The high-resolution O 1s XPS spectra are shown in Fig. 4C. For pristine TiO2 and 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x, the O 1s peak can be fitted into two peaks related to the TiO bond and the oxygen vacancy [24,31], respectively. The presence of a small amount of oxygen vacancies in pristine TiO2 is mainly attributed to the defects naturally formed during the preparation process. However, compared with the pristine TiO2, the intensity of the oxygen vacancy in 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x is increased significantly, suggesting the formation of more oxygen vacancies in the nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 4D, there is no peak in the high-resolution N 1s XPS spectra for pristine TiO2. Meanwhile, in the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite, the main peak at 399.56 eV was observed and can be fitted into two peaks with binding energies of 399.51 eV and 400.25 eV, which are assigned to the OTiN bond and the TiON bond [31,32], respectively. The above results suggest that the developed facile synthetic method can achieve the introduction of CDs and the formation of nitrogen and Ti3+/Ov codoping levels into TiO2 at the same time.

In Fig. 5 A, the photoreduction of Cr(VI) by the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite is compared with that by several TiO2-based photocatalysts, including the pristine TiO2, N-doped TiO2 (N-TiO2), N and Ti3+ codoped TiO2 (N-TiO2-x), and carbon quantum dots-TiO2 (CDs-TiO2). Due to the major drawbacks of TiO2, i.e., the poor visible-light absorption and rapid charge recombination [43], the concentration of Cr(VI) barely decreased over pristine TiO2. In contrast, the N-TiO2-x, 5% CDs-TiO2 and 5% CDs-TiO2-x nanocomposites exhibited a moderate photocatalytic performance, resulting in 29%, 30% and 61% of Cr(VI) removal efficiency, respectively. As expected, the novel CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposites exhibited outstanding photocatalytic activities for the remediation of Cr(VI) compared to the other photocatalysts. Obviously, the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) by the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was improved significantly from 29% to 48%, 68% and 94%, when the CDs doping level increased from 0 to 1%, 3% and 5%, respectively. This result is mainly caused by the unique properties of the CDs, such as excellent photon conversion and charge transfer functions [26]. Thus, with the increasing amount of CDs less than 5%, the enhancement in photoreduction of Cr(VI) by CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposites can be achieved. Meanwhile, a further increase in the CDs amounts from 5% to 10% results in a decrease in the Cr(VI) reduction rate, due to the following two aspects: (1) the excess CDs content (>5%) can result in a competition with N-TiO2-x for the adsorption of visible light [44]; (2) excessive CDs may serve as the recombination centers for the photoinduced electron-hole pairs [24,45]. As observed from Fig. 5D, the characteristic absorption peak of Cr(VI) was gradually weakened as the reaction proceeded, and it almost completely disappeared by the end of the reaction, indicating that the highly toxic Cr(VI) species have been completely removed by the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite.

To more reasonably evaluate the photocatalytic activities of all catalysts, the adsorption abilities of Cr(VI) by all samples were investigated (Fig. 5B). The pristine TiO2 exhibited the highest adsorption amount of Cr(VI) at approximately 44.95%. This could be attributed to the fact that the isoelectric point of pristine TiO2 is 7.5 [46]. Since the pristine TiO2 is positively charged at a pH of 5.36, the coulomb attraction between Cr2O7 2− and TiO2 benefits the adsorption. In contrast, all the modified samples exhibited relatively low adsorption abilities. Moreover, the introduction of CDs reduced the adsorption capacity of the catalyst. As shown in Fig. 5B, compared with pristine TiO2, the adsorption amount of Cr(VI) by 5% CDs-TiO2 and 5% CDs-TiO2-x decreased from 44.95% to 39.10% and 37.04%, respectively. Similarly, the adsorption amounts of Cr(VI) by the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposites decreased from 29.37% to 29.02%, 27.17%, 26.95%, and 20.68% as the amounts of CDs increased from 0 to 1%, 3%, 5%, and 10%, respectively. Under this situation, however, the Cr(VI) reduction rate by CDs-TiO2 and CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposites remained much higher than that of the other photocatalysts, which indicates that the decrease in adsorption ability barely affects the reduction rate of Cr(VI) over the modified catalysts.

To study the kinetics of Cr(VI) reduction over as-prepared samples, the data of Fig. 5A were fitted by the pseudo-first-order rate expression (Eq. (2)):
(2)
ln (
C t
/
C 0
) = - kt
where Ct and C0 are the concentration of residual Cr(VI) at time t and time zero, respectively, and k is the apparent reaction rate constant. As shown in Fig. S2, the kinetic curves of all catalysts were linear and all linear regression coefficients (R2) exceeded 97% (Table S1), demonstrating that the pseudo-first-order kinetic model has a good fit to the experimental data. Thus, values of the rate constant for Cr(VI) reduction by different photocatalysts are provided in Fig. 5C. It is observed that the apparent rate constant for Cr(VI) reduction by the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was up to 0.0421 min−1, which is about 2.84-, 7.39-, 8.59-, 32.38- and 105.25-fold faster than that of CDs-TiO2-x, CDs-TiO2, N-TiO2-x, N-TiO2 and pristine TiO2, respectively. In addition, the reaction rate constant for Cr(VI) reduction by the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was much higher than the sum of the apparent rate constants over the CDs-TiO2-x and N-TiO2-x nanocomposites. Therefore, we further utilized the synergistic index (SI) to evaluate the synergistic effect of CDs-modification and N/Ti3+ codoping in the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite. According to the previous study [47], SI of the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite can be calculated via Eq. (3):
(3)
SI =
k
( C D s - N - T i O 2 - x )
k
C D s - T i O 2 - x
+
k
( N - T i O 2 - x )
where k (CDs-N-TiO2-x), k (CDs-TiO2) and k (N-TiO2-x) are the corresponding reaction rate constants for Cr(VI) reduction over the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x, 5% CDs-TiO2 and N-TiO2-x nanocomposites. From the calculation formula of SI, we observed that if there is no synergy and only a simple superposition of multiple components in the reaction system, the calculated SI should be equal to or close to 1.0. In contrast, values of SI greater than 1.0 indicate that there is synergy in the reaction system, and the value exceeding 1.0 corresponds to the increase in the reaction rate caused by the synergistic effect. As a result, SI for the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was calculated to be 2.14, indicating that the synergistic effect of CDs-modification and N/Ti3+ codoping can significantly improve the photoreduction ability of the nanocomposite by 114%. Furthermore, Table 1 indicates that the developed CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite exhibited the significantly higher Cr(VI) reduction rate than those of most reported visible light photocatalysts.

To prove that the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite reduces Cr(VI) to Cr(III) under visible light irradiation, the concentration changes of Cr(VI) and total chromium in the presence of the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite under visible light and dark conditions were measured. In the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI), the adsorption or reduction of Cr(VI) by the catalyst leads to the decrease in its concentration. As shown in Fig. 5E and F, under visible light irradiation, the concentration of Cr(VI) decreases with the progress of reaction, but remains substantially unchanged under dark conditions. This result indicates that the decrease in Cr(VI) concentration is primarily due to the reduction of the CDs-N-TiO2-x. In addition, there are only two ways to reduce the total chromium concentration. One is the adsorption of Cr(VI) or/and Cr(III) by the catalyst, and the other is reaction of Cr(III) with OH− to form the Cr(OH)3 precipitate. As shown in Fig. 5E and F, only under visible light irradiation does the concentration of total chromium decrease as the reaction progresses. This further proves that Cr(VI) efficiently reduced into Cr(III) by the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite under visible light irradiation.

During the photocatalytic reduction process, upon photoexcitation, electrons transition from the valence band to the conduction band, and generate the same amount of holes in the valence band of the catalyst. If these holes can be consumed in time using a hole scavenger, the recombination of the photogenerated charge carriers will be well suppressed, consequently resulting in the higher photocatalytic activity of Cr(VI) reduction. Solid citric acid was widely utilized as the hole scavenger in previous studies [11,21,22], due to its lower price and smaller storage and transportation facilities compared to the other chemicals. Therefore, citric acid was chosen as the hole-trapping agent to research the influence of a hole scavenger on the removal of Cr(VI) by the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite. As presented in Fig. 6 A and B, the removal rate of Cr(VI) was only 80% without the addition of citric acid after 30 min of reaction. After adding a certain concentration of citric acid, the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) by 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x was significantly improved. In particular, when the citric acid concentration increased to 1.0 mM, the Cr(VI) was completely removed after only 25 min, and the reaction rate constant reached 0.1721 min−1, which is approximately 3.09-fold faster than that of the system without citric acid. However, when the concentration of citric acid was too high (>1.0 mM), the removal rate of Cr(VI) by 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x was inhibited. This result may be mainly attributed to the attachment of excess citric acid to the surface of the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite, generating a barrier between the Cr(VI) species and surface of the catalyst. These results indicate that adding the appropriate concentration of citric acid is very useful for enhancing the photoreduction of Cr(VI) by the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite.

As presented in Fig. 7 A and B, the performance of the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite at Cr(VI) reduction was considerably affected by the initial Cr(VI) concentration. With the increase in the initial Cr(VI) concentration, the rate of Cr(VI) reduction by the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite decreased continuously. When the Cr(VI) concentration was increased from 5 mg/L to 10 mg/L, 20 mg/L, and 40 mg/L, the corresponding rate constant dramatically decreased from 0.2353 min−1 to 0.1721 min−1, 0.0503 min−1, and 0.0144 min−1, respectively. Actually, the above experimental results meet our expectation based on the following considerations. When a certain amount of photocatalyst was used, the number of photoinduced electrons generated by the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was relatively constant during the unit time. The limited amount of photogenerated electrons was not sufficient to reduce the large amount of Cr(VI) species at the high initial concentrations. Therefore, the concentration of the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite may be the key factor in determining the removal rate of Cr(VI) under certain reaction conditions.

As observed in Fig. 8 A and B, the photoreduction of Cr(VI) by 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x was considerably dependent on the catalyst dosage. When the concentration of the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was 0 g/L, the concentration of Cr(VI) did not substantially decrease after 30 min of dark reaction. After adding 0.5 g/L of the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposites, the Cr(VI) reduction rate significantly increased from 0 to 67%. Further increasing the concentration of the catalyst from 0.5 g/L to 1.0 g/L, 2.0 g/L, and 3.0 g/L resulted in a significant improvement in Cr(VI) reduction, and the corresponding reaction rate increased from 0.0354 min−1 to 0.1721 min−1, 0.3099 min−1, and 0.4525 min−1, respectively. This phenomenon could be due to the production of more photoinduced electrons by a higher catalyst dosage, which eventually enhanced the removal of Cr(VI).

As presented in Fig. 9 A and B, the Cr(VI) removal efficiency by the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was also significantly affected by the pH of the initial solution. It was found that the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite reduced Cr(VI) more efficiently under acidic conditions. Obviously, as the pH of the initial solution increased, the removal rate of Cr(VI) by the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite continuously decreased. In particular, when the pH increased to 11.32, a tremendous inhibition of Cr(VI) reduction is observed, and the removal efficiency and rate constant significantly decreased to 45% and 0.0177 min−1, respectively. To better understand and explain this phenomenon, the zeta potential of the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite in the pH rang of 2–10 was measured. As shown in the inset of Fig. 9B, the changing trend of the zeta potential of the catalyst is basically consistent with the changing trend of the reaction rate constants when the pH value increased from 3.07 to 11.32. This result may be attributed to the increase in the amount of negative surface charge on the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x with increasing pH, which increases the electrostatic repulsion between the catalyst and Cr2O7 2−. Thus, the migration of Cr(VI) to the surface of the catalyst was inhibited, resulting in the lower Cr(VI) removal rate. Moreover, another important reason may be that Cr(VI) is more stable in alkaline conditions. According to the previous studies, Cr(VI) is mainly present as Cr2O7 2− and HCrO4 − under acidic conditions and as CrO4 2− under alkaline conditions [50,51]. The Nernst reduction potential of CrO4 2−/Cr(III) (−0.13 V vs. NHE) is more negative than that of Cr2O7 2−/Cr(III) and HCrO4 −/Cr(III) (1.36 V vs. NHE) [16]. Therefore, it is difficult to reduce Cr(VI) under alkaline conditions because of the small thermodynamic driving force. Under acidic conditions, the Nernst reduction potential of Cr2O7 2−/Cr(III) and HCrO4 −/Cr(III) will increase continuously as the pH decreases [16], resulting in an increase of the thermodynamic driving force for the reactions. Therefore, Cr(VI) can be more efficiently reduced by CDs-N-TiO2-x at lower pH values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal and chemical stability of the catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to having an excellent photocatalytic performance, an ideal photocatalyst should also have great thermal and chemical stability. Therefore, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and the cyclic tests of 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x were conducted. As observed from the TGA curve (Fig. 10 A), the as-prepared 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite has great thermal stability. Below 200 °C, the 6.5% weight loss is primarily due to the evaporation of water adsorbed by the catalyst [24]. The 5.4% weight loss observed between 200 °C and 550 °C should result from the slow combustion of CDs, which is consistent with the designed CDs content of the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite. Moreover, to prove that the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite has good chemical stability and reusability for Cr(VI) reduction, the catalyst was separated from the reaction system after each experiment by centrifugation and washed with 1 M HNO3 aqueous solution and ultrapure water several times to remove the Cr(OH)3 deposits on the catalyst [22]. After drying at 60 °C under a vacuum, it was used for the next cycle photoreduction experiment. As observed in Fig. 10B, the 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite could be reused up to five photocatalysis cycles with only a slightly decreased catalytic performance, indicating that the as-prepared CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite has great chemical stability.

According to the above results and discussions, the enhanced reduction of Cr(VI) by CDs-N-TiO2-x can be attributed to the synergistic effect of CDs-modification and N/Ti3+ codoping in the nanocomposite. On the one hand, due to the unique photon-conversion property, CDs can serve as an excellent component for improving the visible light absorption of a photocatalyst. However, as observed from Fig. 3C, after absorbing visible light with a wavelength of 500–900 nm, CDs can only convert it in the range of 400–500 nm. This result means that only those photocatalysts that can absorb light with a wavelength greater than 400 nm can be effectively combined with CDs, thereby greatly increasing the utilization of visible light (400–900 nm) for the composite. As shown in Fig. 3B, since the pristine TiO2 can only absorb light with a wavelength below 391 nm, the light absorption ability of the CDs-TiO2 nanocomposite has not been improved significantly. In contrast, for N and Ti3+ codoped TiO2 (N-TiO2-x), the formation of N doping levels above the valence band and the Ti3+ or oxygen vacancies (Ti3+/Ov) underneath the conduction band is beneficial for narrowing the band gap. As a consequence, the light absorption edge of the N-TiO2-x composite has been effectively extended to 441 nm. Thus, after decorating the CDs, the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite could utilize a broad range of the visible light.

On the other hand, in addition to the enhanced light absorption, efficient charge separation is also very crucial for improving the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst. To better understand the mechanism of efficient reduction of Cr(VI) by the novel CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite, the recombination of charge carries in all catalysts were studied by steady-state PL analysis. In general, the higher the PL intensity, the more serious the recombination of charge carriers in a catalyst is [52,53]. As observed from Fig. 11 A, compared with other catalysts, the PL intensity of the 5% CDs-TiO2 nanocomposite was significantly reduced, which indicates that CDs can promote the separation of electron-hole pairs effectively [24,28,30,54]. For the N-TiO2-x composite, its PL intensity decreased sharply compared with that of the pristine TiO2 and N-TiO2, which indicates that the modifications of Ti3+/Ov doping can also accelerate the charge separation of the catalyst. As a consequence, the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite exhibited a lower PL intensity than the other catalysts, demonstrating that the separation of the charge carriers in the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was effectively enhanced. Moreover, Fig. 11B shows the time-resolved PL spectra of pristine TiO2, N-TiO2-x and CDs-N-TiO2-x. By multi-exponential fitting (Table S2), the lifetime of the excited electrons in CDs-N-TiO2-x (2.17887 ns) is longer than that in pristine TiO2 (1.00632 ns) and N-TiO2-x (1.38955 ns), confirming the efficient charge separation of the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite.

Based on the above analysis, the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite may produce abundant photoinduced electrons for Cr(VI) reduction benefits from the enhanced light absorption and charge separation. To further confirm the above speculation, transient photocurrent response experiments under visible light were performed. As observed in Fig. 11C, the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite exhibited the highest photocurrent density, implying that the large amount of electrons generated in CDs-N-TiO2-x can be effectively separated and then efficiently utilized for the removal of Cr(VI). Hence, the feasible mechanism for enhanced removal of the Cr(VI) by CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite can be specified in Fig. 11D and the following procedures:
(4) CDs-N-TiO2-x + hν(λ > 420 nm) → e− + h+
(5) Cr2O7 2− + H+ + e− → Cr3+ + H2O
(6) h+ + hole scavenger → byproducts
(7) Cr3+ + 3OH− → Cr(OH)3 ↓

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in the treatment of real laboratory wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: Chemical oxygen demand (COD) is an important indicator of the organic pollutants in water. In our laboratory, a large amount of wastewater can be generated during the measurement of COD, which mainly contains K2Cr2O7, HgSO4, Ag2SO4, and H2SO4, etc. This kind of real laboratory wastewater has a Cr(VI) concentration of 80–100 mg/L, and its pH value is 1–2. The CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite exhibited a strong ability to reduce Cr(VI) under visible light, and the reduction efficiency was better under acidic conditions. Therefore, we attempted to utilize the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite as a promising photocatalyst for the remediation of the real laboratory wastewater. As shown in Fig. 12 , the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite also exhibited a higher Cr(VI) removal efficiency (100%) than the other photocatalysts, indicating that this novel nanocomposite can be an alternative photocatalyst for the efficient and low-cost treatment of Cr(VI) contained in real laboratory wastewater.

In conclusion, the novel CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite with enhanced visible light-driven photoreduction abilities was successfully realized via a facile synthetic route. The main findings and conclusions are as follows:
(1)
The utilization of visible light and the separation of charge carriers in the CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite was significantly promoted, due to the effect of CDs loading, Ti3+/Ov and nitrogen codoping.
(2)
The 5% CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite exhibited the best performance at Cr(VI) reduction with a reaction rate constant of approximately 0.0421 min−1, which is approximately 7.39- and 8.59-fold faster than that of the reported CDs-TiO2 and N-TiO2-x nanocomposites, respectively.
(3)
Adding the appropriate amount of hole scavenger (citric acid) significantly improved the removal rate of Cr(VI) by CDs-N-TiO2-x. Moreover, the Cr(VI) removal rate was better under acidic conditions and considerably affected by the initial concentration of Cr(VI) and catalyst dosage.
(4)
The CDs-N-TiO2-x nanocomposite also exhibited a higher removal rate of Cr(VI) in real laboratory wastewater than the other photocatalysts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Project (Grant No. 2016YFC0400701), the Program for Innovative Research Team in Shaanxi Province of China (Grant No. 2013KCT-13), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51808434), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (Program No. 2018JQ5129), and the Chinese Government “Hundred Talent Program of Shaanxi Provence”.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2018.09.172.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) over titania suspensions

Carbon quantum dots decorated MoSe2 photocatalyst for Cr(VI) reduction in the UV-vis-NIR photon energy range
